title: Elastic and structural anisotropy in silica thin films for gravitational-wave detectors
authors: Brenda Bracco, Michele Magnozzi, Stefano Colace, Maurizio Canepa, Giulio Favaro, Marco Bazzan, Massimo Granata, David Hofman, Alessandro Di Michele, Laura Silenzi, Gianpietro Cagnoli, Giovanni Carlotti, Paola Sassi, Silvia Corezzi
abstract: The thermal noise of mirror coatings for gravitational-wave detectors critically depends on the elastic properties of the constituent materials. Data analyses and theoretical models typically assume each material is homogeneous and isotropic, but isotropy has never been explicitly verified. Using Brillouin light scattering (BLS), we demonstrate for the first time that ion-beam-sputtered SiO2 -- a material still viable for future mirror coatings -- exhibits cylindrical elastic symmetry, with in-plane isotropy but a notable 6% compressive anisotropy along the film normal. This anisotropy remains unchanged after the post-deposition heat treatment currently used in ground-based detectors (500 $^\circ$C, 10 h) but is nearly eliminated at 900 $^\circ$C. Infrared reflectivity experiments support these findings by directly revealing heterogeneities in the distribution of bridging and non-bridging oxygen structures along the growth axis. While BLS measures the real part of the elastic constants at GHz frequencies, the data reveal negligible contributions from mechanical relaxations in the kHz-GHz range, making BLS a valid substitute for low-frequency properties obtained from standard anisotropy-insensitive techniques. Our results highlight that restoring isotropy through heat treatment -- by softening the material, enabling more than 7% out-of-plane expansion, and smoothing out structural heterogeneities -- may play a key role in reducing thermal noise. This proof-of-concept study extends beyond silica, providing critical insights for the design of future coatings.
summary: This paper demonstrates that silica thin films used in gravitational-wave detectors are not isotropic but exhibit significant compressive anisotropy along the growth axis. It shows that while standard annealing does not fix this, high-temperature treatment can restore isotropy, potentially reducing thermal noise in future detectors.
main_problem: The study investigates whether the assumed elastic isotropy in ion-beam-sputtered silica thin films is valid, as unmodeled anisotropy may contribute to thermal noise in gravitational-wave detectors.
main_result: The researchers discovered that as-deposited silica films exhibit cylindrical elastic symmetry with a 6% compressive anisotropy along the film normal, which can be nearly eliminated through high-temperature (900 C) annealing.
method: The study uses Brillouin Light Scattering (BLS) to probe GHz-frequency elastic constants, alongside Infrared (IR) reflectivity, X-ray Reflectivity (XRR), and Spectroscopic Ellipsometry (SE) to characterize structural and optical properties.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of ion-beam-sputtered (IBS) SiO2 thin films on silicon or sapphire substrates, modeled as transversely isotropic media with five independent elastic constants.
key_observables: Elastic constants (c11, c33, c44, c66, c13), acoustic wave velocities, refractive index, mass density, and oxygen structure distribution (bridging vs. non-bridging).
important_parameters: GHz-range acoustic frequencies, annealing temperatures (as-deposited, 500 C, and 900 C), and the ratio of film thickness to phonon wavelength.
main_assumptions: The study compares GHz-range BLS measurements to kHz-range mechanical properties, assuming negligible mechanical relaxations in the frequency range between the two.
figures_summary: Figures include schematics of BLS backscattering geometry and acoustic wave types, IR spectroscopy mode configurations, and various spectra/plots for XRR, ellipsometry, and elastic constant evolution.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of thermal noise in GW detectors, describes the experimental setup for BLS and IR spectroscopy, presents the discovery of elastic anisotropy, analyzes the effects of thermal annealing on structural and elastic properties, and concludes with implications for coating design.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Schematic diagram of the backscattering geometry used for BLS measurements. The wavevectors of incident, re- fracted, and reflected light are indicated in red, the wavector of scattered light in green, and the wavevectors of bulk-like and surface-like acoustic waves responsible for backscattered light in blue. All wavevectors lie within the scattering plane. In the experimental setup, the sample is mounted vertically, the scattering plane is horizontal, and s- or p-polarized in- coming light is used.

FIG. 2: Schematic view of the four types of acoustic waves detectable in BLS spectra from SiO2 thin films deposited on a reflective substrate. For each acoustic wave, the propaga- tion direction is indicated by a blue vector, and the particle motion is represented by a red symbol. The polarization of the scattered light is indicated below each image.

FIG. 3: Schematic representation of TO and LO modes (red arrows) probed by s- and p-polarized IR radiation. At normal incidence (left panel), IR spectroscopy detects only the trans- verse (TO) component of the antisymmetric Si-O-Si stretch- ing vibration. At oblique incidence (right panel), both the transverse (TO) and longitudinal (LO) components can be observed.

FIG. 4: (a) Polarized (s-s) and depolarized (s-p) BLS spectra of the as-deposited sample S21089 at selected incidence angles θ. The Rayleigh, longitudinal (surface-like and bulk), and shear horizontal peaks are labeled as RW, LM, LB, and SHM, respectively. (b) Lower frame: Brillouin frequency fBLS of different surface-like acoustic waves as a function of the incidence angle. Solid spheres are data for the sample S21089, stars are data for the thinner samples S21088 and S19175. Solid lines represent linear fits through the origin. Error bars are smaller than the symbol size. Upper frame: Brillouin frequency fLB of bulk-like acoustic wave as a function of the incidence angle, from s-s...

FIG. 5: (a) Dependence on the annealing temperature of the refractive index n532 obtained from the SE isotropic model and the BLS-apparent refractive index nBLS. (b) Dependence of the sound velocities vLM, vSHM, vRW , and vLB on the annealing temperature, with vLB measured at quasi-normal incidence. (c) Dependence on the annealing temperature of the elastic constants c11, c33, c66, and c44. (d) Dependence of the quantity (n2 532 −1)/(n2 532 + 2) on the film density ρ, and best fit with the Lorentz-Lorenz equation (solid line). The legend is the same for all the panels. Dashed lines are just a visual guide.
